title: Design Patterns in Practice
summary: Apply Gang of Four design patterns — Singleton, Factory, Strategy, Observer — in real Python projects

sections.title: Design patterns are proven blueprints for common problems
sections.content: Design patterns are reusable solutions to recurring design problems — like architectural templates for common building layouts. They're not code you copy-paste but strategies you adapt. The Singleton pattern ensures a class has exactly one instance (like a single database connection pool). The Factory pattern decouples object creation from usage (like a car factory that produces different models based on options). The Strategy pattern lets you swap algorithms at runtime (like a GPS choosing different route strategies: fastest, shortest, or scenic). The Observer pattern notifies multiple objects when something changes (like a YouTube channel notifying subscribers of new videos). Using patterns gives you a shared vocabulary with other developers.

sections.title: Strategy pattern for pricing calculations
sections.content: Different pricing strategies that can be swapped at runtime for an e-commerce system

sections.title: Pattern selection — don't force it
sections.content: The biggest risk with design patterns is over-application — using a pattern because you learned it, not because the problem needs it. Singleton is controversial: many developers consider it an anti-pattern because it introduces global state and makes testing difficult. In Python, modules are already singletons (imported once), so you often don't need a Singleton class. Factory patterns shine when object creation is complex (multiple steps, configuration-based, or conditional). Strategy is ideal when you have multiple algorithms for the same task that might change at runtime. Observer is essential for event-driven systems. Always ask: 'does this simplify or complicate my code?'

sections.title: Singleton in Python is often unnecessary
sections.content: Many developers overuse Singleton when a module-level variable suffices. Wrong: class Database(metaclass=SingletonMeta): — complex metaclass for global state. Better: in database.py, define connection = create_connection() at module level. Python modules are imported once and cached, naturally acting as singletons. Use a class-based Singleton only when you need lazy initialization or when a framework requires it.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Implement the Observer pattern for a stock price notification system. Create a Stock class (the subject) that maintains a list of observers. Observers implement an update(stock_name, price) method. Create two observers: EmailNotifier and SMSNotifier. When the stock price changes (using a setter), all observers should be notified automatically. Test by changing a stock price and verifying both observers are called.
hints: How do you manage the list of observers that need to be notified?
What method in the subject should iterate and notify all observers?
